context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Concepts: Pro versus Anti-Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do we make of anti-development arguments? Are they utopian or nihilist or neither? Can one be anti-development and still plan? What are the justifications for planning? Or is the division between pro and anti too Manichean? Why certain types of anti-planning arguments emerged at certain times? What was the historical context in which these criticisms emerged? What explains the rise, fall and now the latest rise of anti-development arguments?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I’m kinda lost on this pro vs anti-development reflection tbh. can u just explain the whole thing from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you work through it! Before we dive in — when you hear the word 'development' in the context of non-Western countries, what comes to mind? What do you think it generally means or aims to do?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain everything from the beginning, which would essentially give them the content of the assignment. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to help them discover the key concepts themselves. I'll start by asking what they already know or think about 'development' to get a baseline and help them build from there. Keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: idk, like growth maybe? but ngl I’m confused about basically all of it rn.
exchanges.tutor: Growth is a good starting point! Now think about this: growth for whom, and measured how? Who decides what counts as 'progress' in a country? That tension is really at the heart of the pro vs anti-development debate. What's your instinct?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student gave a minimal but valid starting point - 'growth'. I should affirm this and build on it using guided discovery, pushing them to think about what kind of growth and for whom, which naturally leads into the pro vs anti debate. Keep it short and Socratic.

exchanges.student: tbh that’s where I get stuck, bc it feels like maybe everyone means smth different. can u just give me the big picture first?
exchanges.tutor: Actually, you just said something really insightful — that everyone means something different by development. So who do you think gets to *define* it? And if powerful outsiders define it for a country, what might people inside that country feel about it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking again for me to explain the big picture, which would be giving them the answer. I should resist this and instead push them to engage with their own intuition. They actually had a good insight - that 'everyone means something different' - I should leverage that rather than ignoring it. This is a Socratic opportunity.

exchanges.student: maybe like governments or donors or elites? but then yeah ppl might hate it or not trust it, idk fr.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — and that distrust is precisely what fuels anti-development arguments. So given what you just said, can you take a guess at what an 'anti-development' thinker might argue? What's the core of their objection?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making genuine progress - they've identified key actors (governments, donors, elites) and intuited a legitimacy problem. This is directly relevant to the anti-development critique. I should affirm this and push them to connect it to the actual debate in the assignment - why would someone argue AGAINST development altogether?

exchanges.student: maybe that development is fake or imposed or hurts ppl? I’m still kinda lost tho, like can u explain the whole anti-development side in one go?
exchanges.tutor: You literally just described the anti-development position pretty well! 'Imposed, fake, hurts people' — that's the core of it. Now the prompt asks something specific: are those arguments *utopian* or *nihilist*? What do those two words mean to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me again to explain everything, which I should not do. However, they did produce a genuinely good answer - 'fake, imposed, hurts people' - these are real anti-development arguments. I should affirm this strongly and push them to develop their own thinking further rather than giving them a lecture. I'll connect their answer to the prompt's specific questions to move the conversation forward.

exchanges.student: ngl I’m not even sure about those terms either. can u just walk me through the prompt from start to finish?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but walking you through the whole thing isn't my role — that's your job as the writer! Let's tackle one word at a time though. Forget the academic context for a sec: what does 'utopian' mean to you in everyday language?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking me to walk them through everything, which I must refuse. However, they're expressing genuine confusion about vocabulary, which is a legitimate obstacle I can help with using Socratic questioning rather than lecturing. I should address the specific vocabulary question without giving a lecture, and set clear boundaries about my role.

exchanges.student: like too idealistic maybe? but then I’m also confused abt the history part now, not just the terms.
exchanges.tutor: Yes, utopian = too idealistic, good! So what would the *opposite* of that be — if someone says 'nothing can work, why even try,' what word might describe that attitude? And once we nail these two terms, we can connect them to the history part.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student gave a correct intuition about 'utopian' and is now jumping to another part of the prompt (history). I should affirm their answer on utopian, quickly connect it to nihilist for contrast, and then redirect - they're bouncing around and I should keep them focused on building one idea at a time before moving to history. This prevents shallow engagement.

exchanges.student: maybe nihilist? but tbh I still need the full picture bc the prompt feels all over the place.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — utopian vs nihilist, you got both. Look at what you've already figured out: development is defined by powerful actors, anti-development argues it's imposed and harmful, and critics can be seen as either too idealistic or completely dismissive. That's actually a solid foundation. Try writing 2-3 sentences connecting those ideas — what would you say?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified nihilist. I should affirm this. They've now built both key terms themselves. The student keeps asking for the 'full picture' which I must refuse. However, I notice we've been building concepts piece by piece and the student has actually made good progress - they understand development, anti-development, utopian, and nihilist. I should push them to now try to synthesize what they have into a written attempt, since we've had several exchanges and they have enough to start writing something.

exchanges.student: i honestly can’t pull it together yet. can u pls just summarize the full debate super simply?
exchanges.tutor: I won't summarize it for you — that's the essay you need to write! But you already have the pieces. Just try ONE sentence: why might someone argue against development? Use your own words from earlier in our conversation.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me again to summarize everything for them. This is the fourth or fifth time they've asked for a full explanation, which I must refuse. However, they're expressing genuine difficulty synthesizing. Rather than summarizing, I should be more direct about my role, and then give them a very targeted nudge - pointing them back to what they've already said and asking them to just write ONE sentence, not 2-3, to lower the barrier.

exchanges.student: maybe bc “development” can be imposed by powerful outsiders and end up harming the ppl it claims to help?
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong sentence — hold onto it, it could open your essay. Now the prompt also asks *when and why* these anti-development arguments emerged historically. What global events or shifts do you think might have made people start questioning development as a concept?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is a genuinely strong sentence - clear, accurate, and in their own words. I should affirm it enthusiastically. We're at exchange 9 of 10, so I should push them to now connect this to the historical context question from the prompt, which they haven't touched yet, and encourage them to start drafting.
